title: Chapter 8: Descriptive Statistics for Consultants
text:
After this chapter, you will be able to:

Compute and interpret mean, median, and standard deviation for MNH indicators across countries and facility types
Build proportion tables and interpret them in consulting context
Construct and interpret confidence intervals using t.test() — understanding what the interval does and does not tell you
Compute correlation with cor.test() and explain why correlation does not imply causation
Fit a linear regression with lm(), interpret coefficients with summary(), and visualize the fitted line
Distinguish between statistical significance (p-value) and practical significance (effect size)

Fit a linear regression with lm(), interpret coefficients with summary(), and visualize the fitted line

title: Chapter 8: Descriptive Statistics for Consultants
text:
Key Concepts

Central Tendency: Mean vs Median
The mean is the arithmetic average — sensitive to outliers. The median is the middle value — robust to outliers. For skewed distributions (common in health data: a few facilities with very high NMR), the median better represents the “typical” facility. Always report both, and explain which one you are using and why.


Confidence Intervals
A 95% confidence interval means: if you repeated the study many times, 95% of the intervals you compute would contain the true population parameter. It quantifies uncertainty around your estimate. A narrow interval means you have a precise estimate; a wide interval means you need more data. Consultants should report CIs alongside point estimates, especially when sample sizes are small.


Correlation vs Causation
cor.test() measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two variables. A correlation of -0.7 between CPAP availability and NMR means they tend to move in opposite directions. But this alone does not prove that adding CPAP machines causes NMR to decline — confounders (hospital capacity, staffing) could explain the pattern. This connects directly to the causal reasoning framework from the Causal Arguments course.


Linear Regression
lm(nmr ~ deliveries + facility_type, data = df) fits a line predicting NMR from delivery volume and facility type. The coefficients tell you: “For each additional delivery, NMR changes by X, holding facility type constant.” Regression is the quantitative foundation for the causal models you will build in the Inference and Intervention course.


Statistical vs Practical Significance
A p-value < 0.05 means the result is unlikely due to chance alone. But a statistically significant effect can be practically meaningless: a program that reduces NMR by 0.1 per 1,000 may be statistically significant with a large sample but irrelevant for policy. Always ask: “Is the effect big enough to matter for the decision at hand?”

title: Chapter 8: Descriptive Statistics for Consultants
text:
R Code Workshop
This workshop walks through the statistical tools a consultant needs to summarize data, quantify uncertainty, and test relationships — all with MNH examples.

Summary Statistics by Country

library(tidyverse)

# Simulated facility-level data
set.seed(42)
facility_data <- tibble(
  facility_id   = paste0("F", 1:60),
  country       = rep(c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"), each = 20),
  facility_type = sample(c("hospital", "health_center", "health_post"),
                          60, replace = TRUE,
                          prob = c(0.2, 0.4, 0.4)),
  live_births   = round(runif(60, 50, 500)),
  neonatal_deaths = round(runif(60, 0, 20)),
  has_cpap      = sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), 60, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.3, 0.7))
) |>
  mutate(nmr = neonatal_deaths / live_births * 1000)

# Summary statistics by country
facility_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_nmr   = round(mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    median_nmr = round(median(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    sd_nmr     = round(sd(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    min_nmr    = round(min(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    max_nmr    = round(max(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
  )

Notice how the mean and median can differ substantially. When they diverge, it usually means the distribution is skewed — a few facilities with very high (or very low) NMR are pulling the mean away from the center. In your client deliverables, report the median for skewed distributions and explain why.


Proportion Tables

# What proportion of facilities have CPAP equipment?
facility_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    n_facilities = n(),
    n_with_cpap  = sum(has_cpap),
    pct_cpap     = round(mean(has_cpap) * 100, 1)
  )

# Cross-tabulation: facility type by CPAP availability
facility_data |>
  count(facility_type, has_cpap) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = has_cpap, values_from = n,
              names_prefix = "cpap_") |>
  mutate(pct_with_cpap = round(cpap_TRUE / (cpap_TRUE + cpap_FALSE) * 100, 1))

Proportion tables are the building blocks of consulting deliverables. “Only 30% of health posts have CPAP equipment, compared to 80% of hospitals” is a finding that drives resource allocation decisions. Always pair proportions with absolute counts so your client understands both the rate and the scale.


Confidence Intervals with t.test

# 95% confidence interval for mean NMR in Ethiopia
ethiopia_nmr <- facility_data |>
  filter(country == "Ethiopia") |>
  pull(nmr)

t_result <- t.test(ethiopia_nmr)
t_result

# Extract the key numbers
t_result$estimate     # point estimate (mean)
t_result$conf.int     # 95% CI bounds

# CI by country
facility_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    mean_nmr = mean(nmr),
    ci_lower = t.test(nmr)$conf.int[1],
    ci_upper = t.test(nmr)$conf.int[2],
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 1)))

A confidence interval tells you the range of plausible values for the true mean. If the CI for Ethiopia is [18.2, 32.4], you can say: “We estimate the mean NMR across Ethiopian facilities in our sample is between 18.2 and 32.4 per 1,000 live births.” Wider intervals mean more uncertainty — usually because the sample size is small or the variation is large.


Correlation Analysis

# Is delivery volume correlated with NMR?
cor.test(facility_data$live_births, facility_data$nmr)

# Visualize the relationship
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = live_births, y = nmr)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = country), size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#005CB9", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Delivery Volume vs Neonatal Mortality",
    subtitle = "Correlation does not imply causation",
    x = "Live Births (annual)",
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    color = "Country"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The cor.test() output gives you three things: the correlation coefficient (r), the p-value, and a confidence interval. But even a strong correlation does not prove causation. Higher-volume facilities may have lower NMR because they are better-resourced hospitals — not because volume itself reduces mortality. This is exactly the kind of reasoning you practiced in the Causal Arguments course.


Linear Regression

# Fit a linear model: NMR as a function of delivery volume and CPAP
model <- lm(nmr ~ live_births + has_cpap + facility_type,
            data = facility_data)

# Interpret the results
summary(model)

# Key coefficients to report:
# - Intercept: estimated NMR when all predictors are at reference level
# - live_births: change in NMR for each additional birth
# - has_cpapTRUE: difference in NMR between facilities with/without CPAP
# - facility_type: difference relative to reference category

The summary(model) output shows estimated coefficients, standard errors, t-values, and p-values. Focus on the Estimate column (effect size) and the Pr(>|t|) column (significance). But always ask: is the effect practically meaningful? A coefficient of -0.002 on live_births means NMR decreases by 0.002 per additional birth — statistically significant perhaps, but negligible for policy.


Visualizing Regression Results

# Visualize the regression with confidence band
ggplot(facility_data, aes(x = live_births, y = nmr, color = has_cpap)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("TRUE" = "#83BD00", "FALSE" = "#E24A3F"),
    labels = c("TRUE" = "Has CPAP", "FALSE" = "No CPAP")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "NMR by Delivery Volume and CPAP Availability",
    subtitle = "Linear regression with 95% confidence bands",
    x = "Live Births (annual)",
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    color = "CPAP Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

This chart shows two regression lines: one for facilities with CPAP and one without. If the lines have different intercepts, CPAP availability is associated with different NMR levels even after accounting for delivery volume. The shaded bands show uncertainty — wider bands mean fewer data points in that range.

title: Chapter 8: Descriptive Statistics for Consultants
text:
Key Takeaways

Report mean, median, and confidence intervals together. The mean summarizes, the median resists outliers, and the CI quantifies uncertainty. Your client needs all three to make informed decisions.
Correlation measures association, not causation. Use cor.test() to quantify relationships, but remember that confounders can create misleading correlations — refer back to the causal reasoning framework.
Regression is a tool, not an answer. lm() tells you which variables are associated with NMR and by how much, but the coefficients are only as good as the model specification. Always consider what you might be missing.

title: Chapter 6: Data Visualization with ggplot2
text:
Key Concepts

Grammar of Graphics
Every ggplot2 chart is built from three components: data (the tibble), aesthetics (which variables map to which visual properties — x-axis, y-axis, color, size), and geoms (the geometric shapes that represent the data — bars, points, lines). You layer these components with + to build up a visualization step by step.


Geom Types








Geom
Use Case
MNH Example




geom_col()
Compare categories
NMR by country


geom_point()
Show relationships
Deliveries vs. NMR


geom_line()
Show trends over time
Quarterly NMR trends


geom_histogram()
Show distributions
Distribution of facility NMRs


geom_boxplot()
Compare distributions
NMR by facility type





Aesthetic Mappings
Aesthetics connect data to visual properties. aes(x = country, y = nmr) puts countries on the x-axis and NMR on the y-axis. aes(color = region) colors points by region. Aesthetics inside aes() are data-driven (they vary with the data); aesthetics outside aes() are fixed (e.g., color = "blue" makes everything blue).


Chart Selection
The right chart depends on your question: Comparing categories? Bar chart. Showing a relationship between two numbers? Scatter plot. Tracking change over time? Line chart. Understanding a distribution? Histogram or boxplot. Choosing the wrong chart type obscures your message; choosing the right one makes the insight immediately visible.

title: Chapter 6: Data Visualization with ggplot2
text:
R Code Workshop
This workshop builds five core chart types using MNH data. Each chart answers a different analytical question a consultant might face.

Bar Chart: NMR by Country

library(tidyverse)

# Country-level NMR data
country_nmr <- tibble(
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Kenya", "Rwanda"),
  nmr = c(28.1, 20.3, 22.4, 18.6, 16.2)
)

# Bar chart — compare a numeric value across categories
ggplot(country_nmr, aes(x = reorder(country, nmr), y = nmr)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#005CB9") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Neonatal Mortality Rate by Country",
    subtitle = "Deaths per 1,000 live births",
    x = NULL,
    y = "NMR"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

reorder(country, nmr) sorts the bars by NMR value rather than alphabetically — this makes the comparison immediate. coord_flip() makes horizontal bars, which are easier to read when category labels are long (country or facility names).


Scatter Plot: Delivery Volume vs NMR

# Facility-level data
facility_scatter <- tibble(
  facility = paste0("F", 1:20),
  country = sample(c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"), 20, replace = TRUE),
  total_deliveries = c(320, 85, 410, 120, 250, 190, 380, 95, 150, 440,
                       60, 210, 330, 175, 290, 105, 360, 225, 145, 400),
  nmr = c(12.5, 28.3, 15.1, 22.7, 18.4, 25.6, 14.2, 31.0, 20.1, 11.8,
          35.2, 19.5, 16.8, 24.1, 17.3, 29.4, 13.5, 21.2, 26.8, 10.9)
)

# Scatter plot — show relationship between two numeric variables
ggplot(facility_scatter, aes(x = total_deliveries, y = nmr, color = country)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(
    title = "Facility Delivery Volume vs Neonatal Mortality",
    x = "Total Deliveries (annual)",
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    color = "Country"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

This chart lets you see whether higher-volume facilities tend to have lower NMR — a pattern you might expect if volume is a proxy for capacity. The color = country aesthetic lets you see whether the pattern holds across countries or varies by context.


Line Chart: NMR Trends Over Time

# Quarterly NMR trends
quarterly_trends <- tibble(
  quarter = rep(c("2023-Q1", "2023-Q2", "2023-Q3", "2023-Q4",
                  "2024-Q1", "2024-Q2"), 3),
  country = rep(c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"), each = 6),
  nmr = c(28.1, 27.3, 26.5, 25.8, 25.1, 24.4,    # Ethiopia: declining
          20.3, 20.1, 19.8, 19.5, 19.2, 18.9,      # Tanzania: declining
          22.4, 23.1, 22.8, 21.9, 22.5, 21.2)       # Malawi: uneven
)

# Line chart — track change over time
ggplot(quarterly_trends, aes(x = quarter, y = nmr,
                              color = country, group = country)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  labs(
    title = "Quarterly NMR Trends by Country",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    color = "Country"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

Line charts require a group aesthetic when you have multiple lines. Without group = country, ggplot2 would try to connect all points in sequence. Adding geom_point() on top of geom_line() makes individual data points visible, which helps when trends are uneven (like the Malawi line).


Histogram: Distribution of Facility NMRs

# Distribution of NMR across many facilities
set.seed(42)
facility_nmrs <- tibble(
  nmr = c(rnorm(50, mean = 22, sd = 6),   # general distribution
          rnorm(10, mean = 35, sd = 4))     # outlier cluster
)

# Histogram — understand the shape of a distribution
ggplot(facility_nmrs, aes(x = nmr)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 3, fill = "#005CB9", color = "white") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 25, color = "#E24A3F", linetype = "dashed",
             linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = 26, y = 12, label = "Target: 25",
           color = "#E24A3F", hjust = 0) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Neonatal Mortality Rates Across Facilities",
    x = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)",
    y = "Number of Facilities"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The dashed red line marks a target threshold. This kind of chart answers the question: “How many of our facilities are above or below the target?” The right tail of the distribution shows outlier facilities with very high NMR — these are the ones to investigate first.


Boxplot: NMR by Facility Type

# NMR by facility type
facility_types <- tibble(
  facility_type = factor(
    rep(c("Health Post", "Health Center", "Hospital"), each = 15),
    levels = c("Health Post", "Health Center", "Hospital")
  ),
  nmr = c(rnorm(15, 30, 5),   # health posts: higher NMR
          rnorm(15, 22, 4),    # health centers: medium
          rnorm(15, 15, 3))    # hospitals: lower NMR
)

# Boxplot — compare distributions across categories
ggplot(facility_types, aes(x = facility_type, y = nmr, fill = facility_type)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#FA7650", "#F8A623", "#005CB9")) +
  labs(
    title = "Neonatal Mortality by Facility Type",
    x = NULL,
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Boxplots show the median (center line), interquartile range (box), and outliers (individual points). They are the best chart for comparing distributions across categories. Here you can see whether hospitals systematically outperform health posts on NMR — and how much variation exists within each type.

title: Chapter 6: Data Visualization with ggplot2
text:
Key Takeaways

Every chart is data + aesthetics + geom. Once you internalize this grammar, you can build any visualization by combining the right components.
Choose chart type by question. Comparing categories = bar chart. Relationships = scatter plot. Trends = line chart. Distributions = histogram or boxplot.
Aesthetics make data visible. Mapping variables to color, size, and shape lets you encode multiple dimensions of information in a single chart — country, facility type, and NMR all in one view.

title: Chapter 4: Data Transformation with dplyr
text:
After this chapter, you will be able to:

Use the pipe operator (|>) to chain data transformation steps into readable pipelines
Apply the five core dplyr verbs: filter(), select(), mutate(), summarise(), and arrange()
Combine group_by() with summarise() to compute grouped statistics (e.g., NMR by country, deliveries by facility type)
Use case_when() to create categorical variables from numeric thresholds
Build a complete analytical pipeline from raw facility data to a summary dashboard table

title: Chapter 4: Data Transformation with dplyr
text:
Key Concepts

The Pipe Operator
The pipe (|> in base R, or %>% from magrittr) takes the result of one function and feeds it as the first argument to the next function. It lets you read code left-to-right, top-to-bottom — like reading a recipe: “Take the data, then filter, then mutate, then summarise.” Without pipes, you either nest functions inside each other (unreadable) or create intermediate variables (cluttered).


The Five dplyr Verbs








Verb
Purpose
Example




filter()
Keep rows that match a condition
Facilities with NMR > 25


select()
Keep or drop specific columns
Only name, country, NMR


mutate()
Create or modify columns
Add a column for NMR category


summarise()
Collapse rows into a summary
Mean NMR across all facilities


arrange()
Sort rows
Sort by NMR, worst first





group_by + summarise
The most powerful pattern in dplyr. group_by(country) splits your data into invisible groups, and summarise() then computes one row per group. This is how you go from facility-level records to a country-level summary table — the foundation of most consulting dashboards.


case_when
A vectorized if-else that maps conditions to values. Use it inside mutate() to create categorical columns from numeric data — for example, classifying facilities into “Low NMR”, “Medium NMR”, and “High NMR” based on threshold values. It evaluates conditions in order and assigns the first match.


Method Chaining as Analytical Thinking
Each step in a dplyr pipeline corresponds to an analytical decision: Which records are relevant? Which variables matter? What new measures do I need? What level of aggregation answers my question? Learning dplyr is not just learning syntax — it is learning to decompose analytical questions into discrete, verifiable steps.

title: Chapter 4: Data Transformation with dplyr

Lecture Slides




Open slides full-screen →

title: Chapter 4: Data Transformation with dplyr
text:
R Code Workshop
This workshop builds a complete analytical pipeline — the kind of summary table you might present in a consulting slide deck — using the five dplyr verbs.

The Pipe in Action

library(tidyverse)

# Without the pipe — nested and hard to read
# arrange(summarise(group_by(filter(facility_data, active == TRUE), country), mean_nmr = mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE)), desc(mean_nmr))

# With the pipe — each step on its own line
facility_data |>
  filter(active == TRUE) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(mean_nmr = mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_nmr))

Read the pipe as “and then.” Take facility_data, and then keep only active facilities, and then group by country, and then compute mean NMR, and then sort from worst to best. Each line is a single, verifiable step.


Filtering and Selecting

# Filter: keep only hospitals with more than 100 deliveries
high_volume <- facility_data |>
  filter(facility_type == "hospital",
         total_deliveries > 100)

# Select: keep only the columns we need
high_volume |>
  select(facility_name, country, total_deliveries, nmr)

# Combine filter + select in one pipeline
facility_data |>
  filter(nmr > 25) |>
  select(facility_name, country, nmr, total_deliveries) |>
  arrange(desc(nmr))

# Use ! to negate: drop facilities with missing NMR
facility_data |>
  filter(!is.na(nmr))

Filtering is your first analytical decision: “Which observations are relevant to my question?” Be explicit about your inclusion criteria. In MNH work, you often need to filter to a specific reporting period, facility type, or country before summarizing.


Creating New Variables with mutate

# Add computed columns
facility_data |>
  mutate(
    # Calculate NMR from raw counts
    nmr_calculated = neonatal_deaths / live_births * 1000,

    # Classify NMR into categories
    nmr_category = case_when(
      nmr < 15  ~ "Low (< 15)",
      nmr < 25  ~ "Medium (15-25)",
      nmr >= 25 ~ "High (25+)",
      TRUE      ~ "Unknown"
    ),

    # Flag facilities above the national target
    above_target = nmr > 20,

    # Calculate percentage of deliveries that are cesarean
    cs_rate = cesarean_deliveries / total_deliveries * 100
  ) |>
  select(facility_name, country, nmr, nmr_category, above_target, cs_rate)

case_when() is especially useful for creating the kind of traffic-light categories that appear in consulting dashboards — green/yellow/red, on-track/at-risk/off-track. The TRUE ~ "Unknown" at the end is the catch-all for any values that do not match the earlier conditions (including NA).


Grouped Summaries: The Country Dashboard

# Build a country-level MNH dashboard
country_dashboard <- facility_data |>
  filter(!is.na(nmr), active == TRUE) |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    n_facilities    = n(),
    total_births    = sum(live_births, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_neo_death = sum(neonatal_deaths, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_nmr        = mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_nmr      = median(nmr, na.rm = TRUE),
    worst_nmr       = max(nmr, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_high_nmr    = mean(nmr > 25, na.rm = TRUE) * 100
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_nmr))

country_dashboard

This is the pipeline pattern you will use most often in consulting work: filter to relevant records, group by the dimension your client cares about (country, region, facility type, quarter), summarise the indicators, and sort to highlight the worst performers. The result is a single table that could go directly into a slide.


Putting It All Together

# Complete pipeline: from raw data to a presentation-ready summary
facility_data |>
  # Step 1: Keep only active facilities with valid data
  filter(active == TRUE, !is.na(nmr), !is.na(total_deliveries)) |>

  # Step 2: Add derived columns
  mutate(
    nmr_category = case_when(
      nmr < 15  ~ "Low",
      nmr < 25  ~ "Medium",
      nmr >= 25 ~ "High"
    ),
    volume_category = case_when(
      total_deliveries < 50  ~ "Low volume",
      total_deliveries < 200 ~ "Medium volume",
      TRUE                   ~ "High volume"
    )
  ) |>

  # Step 3: Summarise by country and NMR category
  group_by(country, nmr_category) |>
  summarise(
    n_facilities = n(),
    avg_deliveries = mean(total_deliveries, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>

  # Step 4: Sort for presentation
  arrange(country, factor(nmr_category, levels = c("High", "Medium", "Low")))

Notice the .groups = "drop" argument in summarise(). After summarizing grouped data, this removes the grouping so subsequent operations apply to the entire result. Forgetting this is a common source of unexpected behavior.

title: Chapter 4: Data Transformation with dplyr
text:
Key Takeaways

The pipe makes your thinking visible. Each line in a dplyr pipeline is one analytical decision. Write your pipelines so a colleague can read them top-to-bottom and understand your logic.
group_by + summarise is your most powerful tool. It transforms facility-level data into the country-level, region-level, or quarter-level summaries that drive consulting recommendations.
case_when creates dashboard categories. Map numeric indicators to human-readable labels — these are the traffic-light classifications your clients expect.

title: Chapter 4: Data Transformation with dplyr
text:
Looking Ahead

title: Chapter 2: R Building Blocks
text:
Key Concepts

Vectors
The most fundamental data structure in R. A vector is an ordered collection of values — all of the same type. When you store NMR values for five countries, that is a numeric vector. When you store country names, that is a character vector. Even a single number in R is technically a vector of length 1.


Data Types
Every value in R belongs to one of several types. The four you will use most: numeric (numbers like 28.1), character (text like "Ethiopia"), logical (TRUE or FALSE), and factor (categorical variables with a fixed set of levels, like facility type). Mixing types in a vector triggers automatic coercion — R silently converts everything to the most general type.


Missing Values (NA)
R uses NA to represent missing data — a facility that did not report, a country with no survey that year, a patient record with an unknown outcome. Most R functions will return NA if any input is NA unless you explicitly tell them to ignore missing values (e.g., mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)). Learning to handle NA properly is essential for working with real-world MNH datasets.


Functions
A named block of code that takes inputs (arguments), performs a calculation, and returns a result. R comes with thousands of built-in functions (mean(), sum(), paste()), and packages add thousands more. You can also write your own functions to encapsulate repeated calculations — like computing NMR or MMR from raw counts.


Getting Help
Every R function has a help page accessed with ?function_name or help(function_name). The help page shows the function’s arguments, what it returns, and usage examples. When you are stuck, ? is your first stop.

title: Chapter 2: R Building Blocks
text:
R Code Workshop
This workshop builds your fluency with R’s core data types and shows you how to write functions that automate MNH indicator calculations.

Creating and Inspecting Vectors

# Numeric vector: NMR by country (per 1,000 live births)
nmr <- c(28.1, 20.3, 22.4, 18.6, 16.2)

# Character vector: country names
countries <- c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Kenya", "Rwanda")

# Logical vector: does the country have NMR above 20?
high_nmr <- nmr > 20
high_nmr  # TRUE TRUE TRUE FALSE FALSE

# Factor vector: facility type (categorical)
facility_type <- factor(c("hospital", "health_center", "hospital",
                           "health_post", "health_center"),
                        levels = c("health_post", "health_center", "hospital"))
facility_type

Notice the logical vector: nmr > 20 tests each element and returns TRUE or FALSE. This is how you will filter data later — by creating logical conditions that select rows.


Type Checking and Conversion

# Check types
is.numeric(nmr)       # TRUE
is.character(countries)  # TRUE
is.logical(high_nmr)  # TRUE

# What happens when types mix?
mixed <- c(1, 2, "three")
mixed  # "1" "2" "three" — everything becomes character

# Convert character to numeric (common with imported data)
reported_values <- c("120", "85", "NA", "200", "150")
as.numeric(reported_values)  # 120 85 NA 200 150

# Convert numeric to factor for categorical analysis
nmr_category <- cut(nmr, breaks = c(0, 15, 25, Inf),
                    labels = c("Low", "Medium", "High"))
nmr_category

Type coercion is one of the most common sources of bugs when importing data. A column that looks numeric in your CSV may be read as character if it contains a single non-numeric entry (like “N/A” or a footnote symbol). Always check your types after import.


Handling Missing Values

# Real-world data has gaps — some facilities did not report
deliveries <- c(340, 215, NA, 180, NA, 420, 310)

# Most functions propagate NA by default
mean(deliveries)      # NA — because R cannot know the true mean
sum(deliveries)       # NA

# Tell R to remove NAs before computing
mean(deliveries, na.rm = TRUE)   # 293
sum(deliveries, na.rm = TRUE)    # 1465

# Count and locate missing values
sum(is.na(deliveries))         # 2 missing values
which(is.na(deliveries))       # positions 3 and 5

# Percentage of data that is missing
mean(is.na(deliveries)) * 100  # 28.6% missing

In MNH data, missing values are not random — facilities that do not report often have the weakest infrastructure. Ignoring NA may bias your results. Always report how much data is missing and consider why it is missing before dropping it.


Writing Custom Functions

# Function to calculate Neonatal Mortality Rate
calc_nmr <- function(neonatal_deaths, live_births) {
  nmr <- neonatal_deaths / live_births * 1000
  return(round(nmr, 1))
}

# Function to calculate Maternal Mortality Ratio
calc_mmr <- function(maternal_deaths, live_births) {
  mmr <- maternal_deaths / live_births * 100000
  return(round(mmr, 0))
}

# Use them
calc_nmr(45, 3200)     # 14.1 per 1,000 live births
calc_mmr(12, 3200)     # 375 per 100,000 live births

# Functions work on vectors too
district_neo_deaths <- c(45, 30, 62, 28, 51)
district_births <- c(3200, 2100, 4500, 1800, 3700)
calc_nmr(district_neo_deaths, district_births)

Writing functions eliminates copy-paste errors. Instead of re-typing the NMR formula in ten different places (and risking a typo in one), you define it once and call it everywhere. If the formula changes, you update one function.


Using the Help System

# Get help on any function
?mean
?round
?cut

# See a function's arguments
args(round)

# Search for functions by keyword
help.search("correlation")

# Example: discovering that mean() has an na.rm argument
?mean
# Usage: mean(x, trim = 0, na.rm = FALSE, ...)
# Setting na.rm = TRUE ignores missing values

The help system is your most reliable reference. Package vignettes (vignette("dplyr")) provide longer tutorials. For complex questions, searching “R [your question]” online will usually lead to a Stack Overflow answer.

title: Chapter 2: R Building Blocks
text:
Key Takeaways

Vectors are the foundation. Every column of data you work with is a vector. Understanding their types (numeric, character, logical, factor) prevents subtle bugs.
NA is not zero. Missing data must be handled explicitly. Always check for NA values and decide whether to remove, impute, or flag them.
Write functions for repeated calculations. If you compute the same indicator more than once, wrap it in a function. This makes your code shorter, more readable, and less error-prone.

# Verification: load each package and print version

tidyverse installation issues on Windows

Install Rtools (match the version to your R version)
Restart R and try install.packages("tidyverse") again



Package installation behind a proxy
If you are on a corporate network with a proxy, set it before installing:

Sys.setenv(http_proxy = "http://your-proxy:port")
Sys.setenv(https_proxy = "http://your-proxy:port")
install.packages("tidyverse")



readxl on older R versions
If readxl installation fails, ensure your R version is 4.3 or later. Update R from r-project.org.

title: R for Management Consultants
text:
This course teaches R programming from scratch, tailored for management consultants working in global health. Across 9 chapters, you will build a complete data analysis toolkit — from writing your first line of R code to producing publication-quality charts and reproducible reports for stakeholders.

The central message: R is not just for statisticians. It is a practical tool that helps consultants answer the questions that matter most: What does the data say? How confident are we? Can we reproduce this analysis next quarter?

title: Chapter 1: Why R for Management Consultants
text:
Key Concepts

Reproducibility
The ability to re-run an analysis and get the same result. In consulting, this means your client can verify your numbers, your team can update a report when new data arrives, and you can trace every figure in a deliverable back to a specific line of code. Spreadsheet-based workflows (copy-paste, manual formatting) break reproducibility.


RStudio IDE
An integrated development environment that wraps around R, providing a code editor with syntax highlighting, a console for interactive commands, an environment pane showing your data objects, and a files/plots/help pane. Think of R as the engine and RStudio as the dashboard.


R Scripts
Plain-text files (.R extension) that contain a sequence of R commands. Scripts are the basic unit of reproducible work — you write your analysis once, save it, and anyone can re-run it. Every consulting analysis should live in a script, not typed directly into the console.


Packages
Extensions that add new functions to R. The base installation handles arithmetic and basic statistics, but packages like tidyverse, readxl, and ggplot2 provide the tools consultants actually need. You install a package once with install.packages() and load it each session with library().

title: Chapter 1: Why R for Management Consultants
text:
R Code Workshop
This workshop walks you through your very first R session — from basic arithmetic to creating variables that hold real MNH indicator data.

Your First Calculations

# R works as a calculator
2 + 2
100 / 4
sqrt(144)

# Neonatal deaths per 1,000 live births (NMR formula)
# If a district had 45 neonatal deaths out of 3,200 live births:
45 / 3200 * 1000

The last line computes a neonatal mortality rate (NMR) of about 14.1 per 1,000 live births. In a spreadsheet you might type this into a cell — in R, you write it as a formula that anyone can read, verify, and re-run.


Creating Variables

# Store values in named variables using the assignment operator <-
neonatal_deaths <- 45
live_births <- 3200

# Now compute NMR from the stored values
nmr <- neonatal_deaths / live_births * 1000
nmr

# Maternal mortality ratio (MMR): maternal deaths per 100,000 live births
maternal_deaths <- 12
mmr <- maternal_deaths / live_births * 100000
mmr

Variable names should be descriptive and use snake_case (lowercase with underscores). This makes your code self-documenting — a colleague reading neonatal_deaths / live_births * 1000 immediately understands the calculation without needing a comment.


Working with Multiple Values

# NMR for five countries (per 1,000 live births)
country_names <- c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Kenya", "Rwanda")
nmr_values <- c(28.1, 20.3, 22.4, 18.6, 16.2)

# R operates element-wise — no loops needed
# Convert NMR to deaths per 10,000 births
nmr_per_10k <- nmr_values * 10
nmr_per_10k

# Which country has the highest NMR?
max(nmr_values)
country_names[which.max(nmr_values)]

The c() function combines values into a vector — R’s fundamental data structure. You will work with vectors constantly. Notice how nmr_values * 10 multiplied every element at once; this vectorized behavior is what makes R powerful for data analysis.


Installing and Loading Packages

# Install the tidyverse (run this ONCE — it downloads from the internet)
# install.packages("tidyverse")

# Load it at the start of every script
library(tidyverse)

# Now you have access to dplyr, ggplot2, readr, and more
# Quick example: create a tibble (modern data frame)
mnh_data <- tibble(
  country = country_names,
  nmr = nmr_values
)
mnh_data

You only need to install a package once (like installing an app), but you must load it with library() every time you start a new R session (like opening the app).

title: Chapter 3: Importing & Exploring Data
text:
Key Concepts

CSV and Excel Import
read_csv() from the readr package (part of tidyverse) reads comma-separated files and automatically guesses column types. read_excel() from the readxl package handles .xlsx and .xls files. Both return tibbles. Always inspect the result immediately after import — column types may not be what you expect.


Tibbles
A tibble is the tidyverse version of a data frame — a rectangular table where each column is a vector and columns can have different types. Tibbles print more neatly (showing only the first 10 rows and as many columns as fit on screen), never convert strings to factors automatically, and warn you when you access a column that does not exist.


Data Exploration
Before any analysis, you must understand your data’s shape and content. glimpse() shows every column’s name, type, and first few values. summary() gives five-number summaries for numeric columns and frequency counts for factors. nrow() and ncol() tell you the dimensions. This exploration step catches data quality issues early.


Missing Values in Imported Data
Real-world health datasets — DHIS2 extracts, facility registers, survey exports — always have missing values. They may appear as blank cells, “NA”, “N/A”, “.”, “-”, or “99”. The read_csv() function recognizes NA and blank cells by default, but you may need to specify additional codes with na = c("", "NA", "N/A", ".", "-"). Always quantify missingness before proceeding.

title: Chapter 3: Importing & Exploring Data

title: Chapter 3: Importing & Exploring Data
text:
R Code Workshop
This workshop simulates importing and exploring a facility-level MNH dataset — the kind of data you might receive from a Ministry of Health or extract from DHIS2.

Importing a CSV File

library(tidyverse)

# Import facility-level MNH data
# In practice, replace the path with your actual file location
facility_data <- read_csv("data/facility_data.csv")

# read_csv prints a column specification showing what types it guessed
# Always check this output for unexpected types

# For Excel files, use readxl
# library(readxl)
# facility_data <- read_excel("data/facility_data.xlsx", sheet = "Sheet1")

# Specify custom NA codes if your data uses them
# facility_data <- read_csv("data/facility_data.csv",
#                           na = c("", "NA", "N/A", ".", "-", "99"))

When you run read_csv(), it prints a message showing which column types it guessed. If a column you expected to be numeric shows as character, that usually means there is a non-numeric value hiding somewhere in that column. Investigate before proceeding.


Exploring Data Structure

# Dimensions: how many rows (facilities) and columns (variables)?
nrow(facility_data)
ncol(facility_data)
dim(facility_data)

# glimpse() — best quick overview (shows every column)
glimpse(facility_data)

# summary() — numeric summaries + NA counts
summary(facility_data)

# First and last few rows
head(facility_data)
tail(facility_data)

# Column names
names(facility_data)

Make glimpse() your first command after every import. It tells you at a glance whether columns have the right types, whether character columns have unexpected values, and how many observations you are working with. In MNH work, always verify that the number of rows matches the expected number of facilities or reporting periods.


Diagnosing Missing Values

# Count NAs in every column
colSums(is.na(facility_data))

# Percentage missing per column
round(colMeans(is.na(facility_data)) * 100, 1)

# Total missing cells in the entire dataset
sum(is.na(facility_data))

# Which rows have any missing data?
facility_data[!complete.cases(facility_data), ]

# How many complete rows (no NAs at all)?
sum(complete.cases(facility_data))

In MNH datasets, missingness is rarely random. Facilities that fail to report are often the ones with the fewest staff, the weakest infrastructure, and potentially the worst outcomes. Before you drop missing rows, ask yourself: “Would removing these facilities bias my results toward healthier-looking facilities?”


Accessing Columns and Creating Tibbles

# Access a single column with $
facility_data$facility_name
facility_data$total_deliveries

# Quick column-level checks
unique(facility_data$country)
table(facility_data$facility_type)
range(facility_data$nmr, na.rm = TRUE)

# Create a tibble from scratch (useful for reference tables)
country_targets <- tibble(
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Kenya", "Rwanda"),
  nmr_target = c(15, 15, 15, 12, 12),
  mmr_target = c(200, 200, 200, 150, 150),
  target_year = rep(2030, 5)
)
country_targets

The $ operator extracts a single column as a vector. The table() function gives you a quick frequency count — useful for checking how many facilities of each type exist, or how many observations come from each country.


Saving Clean Data

# After initial inspection and cleaning, save a clean version
# write_csv(facility_data, "data/facility_data_clean.csv")

# For R-specific format (preserves factor levels, date types, etc.)
# saveRDS(facility_data, "data/facility_data_clean.rds")
# facility_data <- readRDS("data/facility_data_clean.rds")

Save your cleaned data separately from the raw import. This way you always have the original file as a reference, and your cleaning steps are documented in the script that produced the clean version.

title: Chapter 3: Importing & Exploring Data
text:
Key Takeaways

Import with read_csv(), explore with glimpse(). These two functions bookend your data loading workflow. Always inspect column types and dimensions immediately after import.
Quantify missingness before analysis. Count NAs per column, calculate the percentage missing, and ask whether the missingness pattern could bias your results.
Tibbles are your default data container. They print cleanly, warn about mistakes, and integrate seamlessly with the tidyverse tools you will learn next.

title: Chapter 3: Importing & Exploring Data
text:
Looking Ahead
Next in Chapter 4 — Data Transformation with dplyr, you will learn to reshape and summarize your data using five core verbs:

filter() — select rows based on conditions
select() — choose specific columns
mutate() — create or modify columns
summarise() — compute summary statistics
group_by() — apply operations within groups (by country, by facility type, by quarter)

title: Chapter 5: Reshaping & Joining Datasets
text:
Key Concepts

Wide vs Long Format
Wide format has one row per unit (e.g., one row per facility) with separate columns for each time period (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4). Long format has one row per observation (e.g., one row per facility-quarter combination) with a single column for the value. Most R analysis and plotting functions expect long format; most reports and spreadsheets use wide format.


Pivot Operations
pivot_longer() converts wide to long — it gathers multiple columns into key-value pairs. pivot_wider() does the reverse — it spreads key-value pairs into separate columns. These are the two most important data reshaping operations in the tidyverse, and you will use them constantly when working with quarterly DHIS2 data.


Joins
Joins combine two tables based on a shared key column (like facility_id or country_code). left_join() keeps all rows from the left table and adds matching data from the right. inner_join() keeps only rows that match in both tables. anti_join() returns rows from the left table that have no match in the right — invaluable for finding non-reporting facilities.


String Cleaning with stringr
Real-world data has inconsistent text: “Addis Ababa” vs “addis ababa” vs “ADDIS ABABA”, trailing spaces, special characters. The stringr package provides str_to_lower(), str_trim(), str_replace(), and str_detect() to standardize text before joining or grouping. Dirty strings cause silent join failures — rows that should match but do not.


anti_join for Data Quality
anti_join(facilities, reports, by = "facility_id") returns all facilities that did not appear in the reports table. This is the fastest way to find non-reporting facilities, unmatched records, or data gaps. In MNH work, the facilities that fail to report are often the ones most in need of support.

title: Chapter 5: Reshaping & Joining Datasets
text:
R Code Workshop
This workshop covers the two core data wrangling skills that distinguish intermediate R users from beginners: reshaping data between wide and long formats, and joining multiple datasets together.

Pivoting Quarterly Data from Wide to Long

library(tidyverse)

# Simulate quarterly DHIS2 data in wide format
# (This is how data often arrives from health information systems)
dhis2_wide <- tibble(
  facility_id   = c("F001", "F002", "F003", "F004"),
  facility_name = c("Jimma Hospital", "Hawassa HC", "Bahir Dar Hospital", "Mekelle HC"),
  deliveries_q1 = c(320, 85, 410, 120),
  deliveries_q2 = c(345, 90, 395, NA),
  deliveries_q3 = c(310, NA, 420, 115),
  deliveries_q4 = c(330, 88, 435, 125)
)

# Pivot to long format for analysis
dhis2_long <- dhis2_wide |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("deliveries_"),
    names_to = "quarter",
    names_prefix = "deliveries_",
    values_to = "deliveries"
  )

dhis2_long

After pivoting, each facility-quarter combination is its own row. This format is what ggplot2 needs for plotting trends over time, and what group_by(quarter) needs for quarterly summaries. The names_prefix argument strips the “deliveries_” prefix so the quarter column contains just “q1”, “q2”, etc.


Pivoting from Long to Wide for Reports

# Suppose you have a country-level summary in long format
country_summary <- tibble(
  country   = rep(c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"), each = 2),
  indicator = rep(c("NMR", "MMR"), 3),
  value     = c(28.1, 412, 20.3, 321, 22.4, 349)
)

# Pivot to wide format for a report table
country_summary |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = indicator,
    values_from = value
  )

# Pivot quarterly long data back to wide for a spreadsheet export
dhis2_long |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = quarter,
    values_from = deliveries,
    names_prefix = "deliveries_"
  )

Use pivot_wider() when you need to create a table for a report or export to Excel. In consulting, your analysis happens in long format (one observation per row), but your deliverables are often in wide format (one country per row with indicators across columns).


Joining Datasets

# Facility master list
facilities <- tibble(
  facility_id   = c("F001", "F002", "F003", "F004", "F005"),
  facility_name = c("Jimma Hospital", "Hawassa HC", "Bahir Dar Hospital",
                     "Mekelle HC", "Adama Hospital"),
  facility_type = c("hospital", "health_center", "hospital",
                     "health_center", "hospital"),
  country       = c("Ethiopia", "Ethiopia", "Ethiopia",
                     "Ethiopia", "Ethiopia")
)

# Monthly reporting data (F005 did not report)
monthly_reports <- tibble(
  facility_id = c("F001", "F002", "F003", "F004"),
  month       = rep("2024-06", 4),
  deliveries  = c(110, 28, 140, 42),
  neo_deaths  = c(3, 1, 4, 2)
)

# left_join: keep ALL facilities, add report data where available
facilities |>
  left_join(monthly_reports, by = "facility_id")

# inner_join: keep only facilities that reported
facilities |>
  inner_join(monthly_reports, by = "facility_id")

# anti_join: find facilities that did NOT report
facilities |>
  anti_join(monthly_reports, by = "facility_id")

The anti_join at the end is the most important for data quality work. It reveals that Adama Hospital (F005) did not submit a report. In a real engagement, this list of non-reporters is what you would flag to the Ministry of Health or the regional health bureau.


Cleaning Strings Before Joining

# Messy facility names from two different data sources
source_a <- tibble(
  name = c("Jimma University Hospital", "hawassa Health Center",
           " Bahir Dar Hospital", "MEKELLE HC"),
  beds = c(450, 30, 380, 25)
)

source_b <- tibble(
  name = c("jimma university hospital", "Hawassa Health Center",
           "Bahir Dar Hospital", "Mekelle HC"),
  nmr = c(12.5, 18.3, 15.1, 22.7)
)

# Standardize names before joining
clean_name <- function(x) {
  x |>
    str_to_lower() |>       # lowercase everything
    str_trim() |>            # remove leading/trailing spaces
    str_squish() |>          # collapse multiple spaces to one
    str_replace_all("[^a-z0-9 ]", "")  # remove special characters
}

source_a_clean <- source_a |> mutate(name_clean = clean_name(name))
source_b_clean <- source_b |> mutate(name_clean = clean_name(name))

# Now the join works
source_a_clean |>
  left_join(source_b_clean, by = "name_clean") |>
  select(name_clean, beds, nmr)

String cleaning is unglamorous but essential. Without the clean_name() function above, “Jimma University Hospital” and “jimma university hospital” would not match, and you would lose data. Build a cleaning function once and reuse it across all your join operations.


Combining Multiple Operations

# Country-level targets
targets <- tibble(
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"),
  nmr_target = c(15, 15, 15)
)

# Full pipeline: reshape, join, compute, summarise
dhis2_long |>
  # Join with facility master data
  left_join(facilities |> select(facility_id, facility_type, country),
            by = "facility_id") |>
  # Remove missing quarters
  filter(!is.na(deliveries)) |>
  # Summarise by country
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    total_deliveries = sum(deliveries),
    reporting_quarters = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  # Join with targets
  left_join(targets, by = "country")

This pipeline demonstrates the typical consulting workflow: import from multiple sources, reshape to a common format, join on shared keys, and summarise to the level your analysis requires. Each step is readable and verifiable.

title: Chapter 5: Reshaping & Joining Datasets
text:
Key Takeaways

Pivot long for analysis, pivot wide for reports. Most R functions expect long format. Most clients expect wide format. Master both directions.
Joins combine data sources; anti_join finds gaps. In MNH work, the facilities missing from your reports are often the ones that need the most attention.
Clean your strings before joining. Inconsistent capitalization, trailing spaces, and special characters cause silent data loss in joins. Build a reusable cleaning function.

title: Chapter 7: Publication-Quality Charts
text:
Key Concepts

Custom Themes
A ggplot2 theme controls every non-data element of a chart: fonts, grid lines, axis labels, legend position, background color. theme_bka() is a custom function that wraps these settings so you apply them once and every chart in your deliverable looks consistent. Inconsistent formatting across charts signals to clients that the analysis was rushed.


BKA Color Palette
Brand colors are defined once as a named vector and reused everywhere. Using scale_color_manual(values = bka_colors) ensures that “Ethiopia” is always the same shade of blue, “Tanzania” is always teal, and so on. Consistent color mapping across charts helps your audience track countries or categories without re-reading the legend.


Faceting (Small Multiples)
facet_wrap(~ country) creates one panel per country, each with the same axes. Small multiples are the best way to compare patterns across groups — they avoid the visual clutter of overlapping lines and let you see each country’s trajectory clearly. Edward Tufte called them “the best design solution for a wide range of problems in data presentation.”


Patchwork Layouts
The patchwork package lets you combine separate ggplot objects into a single figure using + (side by side) and / (stacked). This is how you create the multi-panel dashboard figures that appear in consulting slide decks — a bar chart on the left, a trend line on the right, with a shared title.


ggsave
ggsave() exports charts as PNG, PDF, or SVG files with explicit control over dimensions and resolution. For slide decks, use width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 300. For print reports, use dpi = 300 minimum. Never screenshot a chart from RStudio — it will be low resolution and inconsistently sized.

title: Chapter 7: Publication-Quality Charts
text:
Lecture Slides




Open slides full-screen →

title: Chapter 7: Publication-Quality Charts
text:
R Code Workshop
This workshop transforms basic ggplot2 charts into polished, brand-consistent visualizations ready for client deliverables.

Building a Reusable Theme

library(tidyverse)

# BK Advisors brand colors
bka_colors <- c(
  "primary"    = "#005CB9",
  "light_blue" = "#00A1DF",
  "green"      = "#83BD00",
  "teal"       = "#3E9B6E",
  "red"        = "#E24A3F",
  "orange"     = "#FA7650",
  "amber"      = "#F8A623",
  "yellow"     = "#FED141"
)

# Named palette for countries (consistent across all charts)
country_colors <- c(
  "Ethiopia" = "#005CB9",
  "Tanzania" = "#3E9B6E",
  "Malawi"   = "#FA7650",
  "Kenya"    = "#83BD00",
  "Rwanda"   = "#00A1DF"
)

# Custom theme function
theme_bka <- function(base_size = 12) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      # Title and subtitle
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = "#005CB9",
                                margin = margin(b = 8)),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "#666666",
                                    margin = margin(b = 12)),
      # Axes
      axis.title = element_text(color = "#333333"),
      axis.text = element_text(color = "#666666"),
      # Grid
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
      # Legend
      legend.position = "bottom",
      legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
      # Plot margin
      plot.margin = margin(15, 15, 15, 15)
    )
}

Define your theme and color palette at the top of every script. This way, every chart you create inherits brand-consistent styling with a single + theme_bka() call. If the brand guidelines change, you update one function.


Applying Brand Colors

# Country-level NMR data
country_nmr <- tibble(
  country = c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Kenya", "Rwanda"),
  nmr = c(28.1, 20.3, 22.4, 18.6, 16.2)
)

# Bar chart with BKA brand styling
ggplot(country_nmr, aes(x = reorder(country, nmr), y = nmr,
                         fill = country)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = country_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Neonatal Mortality Rate by Country",
    subtitle = "Deaths per 1,000 live births | Most recent available year",
    x = NULL,
    y = "NMR"
  ) +
  theme_bka() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")  # Legend redundant with axis labels

Notice how scale_fill_manual(values = country_colors) maps each country to its designated brand color. If you add a new country to the data, add it to the country_colors vector. Keeping color assignments explicit prevents ggplot2 from auto-assigning colors that clash with your brand.


Faceting: Small Multiples

# Quarterly trends by country
quarterly_data <- tibble(
  quarter = rep(paste0("Q", 1:8), 3),
  country = rep(c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"), each = 8),
  nmr = c(28.1, 27.3, 26.5, 25.8, 25.1, 24.4, 23.8, 23.2,  # Ethiopia
          20.3, 20.1, 19.8, 19.5, 19.2, 18.9, 18.5, 18.1,    # Tanzania
          22.4, 23.1, 22.8, 21.9, 22.5, 21.2, 20.8, 21.5),    # Malawi
  target = rep(c(20, 15, 18), each = 8)
)

# Faceted line chart — one panel per country
ggplot(quarterly_data, aes(x = quarter, y = nmr, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(color = "#005CB9", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#005CB9", size = 2) +
  geom_hline(aes(yintercept = target), linetype = "dashed",
             color = "#E24A3F", linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ country, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "NMR Quarterly Trends by Country",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = national target",
    x = "Quarter",
    y = "NMR (per 1,000 live births)"
  ) +
  theme_bka() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, size = 8))

facet_wrap(~ country, scales = "free_y") lets each panel have its own y-axis range, which is appropriate when countries have very different NMR levels. Use scales = "fixed" when you want to emphasize cross-country comparison on the same scale. The choice depends on whether your message is about each country’s trajectory or about the gap between them.


Multi-Panel Layouts with patchwork

library(patchwork)

# Chart 1: Bar chart of current NMR
p1 <- ggplot(country_nmr, aes(x = reorder(country, nmr), y = nmr,
                                fill = country)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = country_colors) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Current NMR", x = NULL, y = "NMR") +
  theme_bka() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

# Chart 2: Line chart of trends (Ethiopia only, for focus)
p2 <- quarterly_data |>
  filter(country == "Ethiopia") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = quarter, y = nmr, group = 1)) +
  geom_line(color = "#005CB9", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(color = "#005CB9", size = 2.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 20, linetype = "dashed", color = "#E24A3F") +
  labs(title = "Ethiopia NMR Trend", x = "Quarter", y = "NMR") +
  theme_bka() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))

# Combine with patchwork
p1 + p2 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "MNH Dashboard: Neonatal Mortality Overview",
    subtitle = "Cross-country comparison and Ethiopia trend detail",
    theme = theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16, color = "#005CB9"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "#666666", size = 12)
    )
  )

The + operator places charts side by side; / stacks them vertically. plot_annotation() adds an overall title. You can also use plot_layout(widths = c(1, 2)) to give more space to the trend chart. This is how you build the multi-panel figures that appear on the “dashboard” slides in consulting decks.


Exporting at Publication Quality

# Save the last plot at high resolution
# ggsave("output/nmr_dashboard.png",
#        width = 14, height = 7, dpi = 300, bg = "white")

# Save as PDF (vector format — scales perfectly at any size)
# ggsave("output/nmr_dashboard.pdf",
#        width = 14, height = 7)

# Save individual charts for different slide layouts
# ggsave("output/nmr_bar_chart.png",
#        plot = p1,
#        width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300, bg = "white")

# Tip: Set bg = "white" to avoid transparent backgrounds
# that look wrong when pasted into PowerPoint

Always specify width, height, and dpi explicitly. The default ggsave() dimensions rarely match your slide layout. For a standard 16:9 slide, width = 10, height = 5.6 is a good starting point. Always use bg = "white" for PNG exports destined for PowerPoint.

title: Chapter 7: Publication-Quality Charts
text:
Key Takeaways

Define your theme once, apply it everywhere. A theme_bka() function ensures every chart in your deliverable looks consistent and professional.
Faceting reveals patterns that overlapping lines hide. Small multiples are especially effective for comparing country-level trends when lines would otherwise cross and tangle.
Export intentionally. Use ggsave() with explicit dimensions and 300 DPI. Never screenshot a chart from RStudio.

After this chapter, you will be able to:

Use code chunk options (echo, eval, warning, fig-width) to control what appears in the rendered output
Insert inline R code to make narrative text update automatically when data changes

title: Chapter 9: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Key Concepts

Quarto Documents
A .qmd file combines three elements: a YAML header (metadata like title, author, format), markdown text (narrative with headings, bold, lists), and code chunks (R code that runs and produces output). When you render the document, Quarto executes the code, captures the results, and weaves them into a polished document. The source file is plain text — version-controllable, reviewable, and reproducible.


Code Chunks
Code chunks are fenced with ```{r} and ```. Chunk options (prefixed with #|) control behavior: echo: true shows the code, eval: true runs it, warning: false hides warnings, fig-width: 8 sets the figure width. You decide what the reader sees: code + output, output only, or code only.


Inline R Code
Inline code uses backtick-r-expression syntax inside markdown text. For example, writing the expression round(mean(nmr), 1) as inline R produces a live number: “The mean NMR was 28.4 per 1,000 live births.” When the data updates, the number in the sentence updates automatically. No more manually updating figures in your narrative — a major source of errors in traditional reporting.


Output Formats
A single .qmd file can render to HTML (for web sharing), PDF (for formal reports), and Word (for client editing). Change the format in the YAML header or render to all three at once. HTML is best for interactive documents; PDF for archival; Word for documents your client will edit.


Parameterized Reports
Quarto documents can accept parameters — variables set in the YAML header that change the document’s behavior. A country brief parameterized with params: {country: "Ethiopia"} filters all data, charts, and narrative to Ethiopia. You can then loop through all countries and render a separate brief for each — producing 10 country reports from a single template.

title: Chapter 9: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Lecture Slides




Open slides full-screen →

title: Chapter 9: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
R Code Workshop
This workshop walks through building an MNH country brief — from document structure to parameterized rendering across all countries.

Document Structure

# A Quarto document (.qmd) looks like this:
#
# ---
# title: "MNH Country Brief"
# author: "BK Advisors"
# date: today
# format: html
# ---
#
# ## Background
#
# This report summarizes neonatal mortality indicators...
#
# ```{r}
# library(tidyverse)
# facility_data <- read_csv("data/facility_data.csv")
# ```
#
# ## Key Findings
#
# The mean NMR was `r round(mean(facility_data$nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1)`
# per 1,000 live births.

# The YAML header (between ---) sets metadata.
# Markdown text provides narrative.
# Code chunks produce analysis and charts.
# Inline R dynamically inserts computed values.

Think of a Quarto document as a script that tells a story. The code produces the evidence (tables, charts, statistics), and the markdown wraps it in narrative context that explains what the numbers mean for your client. When the data updates, you re-render and the entire document updates — narrative included.


Building Summary Tables with kable

library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

# Simulated facility data
set.seed(42)
facility_data <- tibble(
  facility_id   = paste0("F", 1:60),
  country       = rep(c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi"), each = 20),
  facility_type = sample(c("hospital", "health_center", "health_post"),
                          60, replace = TRUE),
  live_births   = round(runif(60, 50, 500)),
  neonatal_deaths = round(runif(60, 0, 20))
) |>
  mutate(nmr = round(neonatal_deaths / live_births * 1000, 1))

# Create a summary table
country_summary <- facility_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(
    Facilities     = n(),
    `Total Births` = sum(live_births),
    `Mean NMR`     = round(mean(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    `Median NMR`   = round(median(nmr, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Render as a formatted table
kable(country_summary,
      caption = "Table 1: MNH Indicators by Country",
      align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r"))

kable() from the knitr package produces clean tables in HTML, PDF, and Word. For more advanced formatting (colors, conditional highlighting, footnotes), use kableExtra. In consulting reports, every table should have a caption (Table 1, Table 2…) and clear column headers.


Inline R for Dynamic Narrative

# Store key numbers for use in inline code
total_facilities <- nrow(facility_data)
total_births     <- sum(facility_data$live_births)
overall_nmr      <- round(
  sum(facility_data$neonatal_deaths) / sum(facility_data$live_births) * 1000,
  1
)
worst_country    <- facility_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(nmr = mean(nmr)) |>
  slice_max(nmr, n = 1) |>
  pull(country)

# In your Quarto document, you would write:
#
# Our analysis covers `r total_facilities` facilities across three
# countries, representing `r format(total_births, big.mark = ",")` live
# births. The overall neonatal mortality rate is `r overall_nmr` per
# 1,000 live births. `r worst_country` has the highest average NMR
# and should be prioritized for intervention.

Inline R is the feature that makes Quarto reports truly reproducible. When new quarterly data arrives, you replace the CSV file, re-render the document, and every number in every sentence updates automatically. No manual find-and-replace, no risk of updating a table but forgetting to update the narrative.


Rendering to Multiple Formats

# YAML header for multi-format output:
#
# ---
# title: "MNH Country Brief"
# author: "BK Advisors"
# date: today
# format:
#   html:
#     toc: true
#     code-fold: true
#   pdf:
#     toc: true
#     number-sections: true
#   docx:
#     toc: true
#     reference-doc: bka-template.docx
# ---
#
# Render from the terminal:
# quarto render country-brief.qmd                  # renders all formats
# quarto render country-brief.qmd --to html        # HTML only
# quarto render country-brief.qmd --to pdf         # PDF only
# quarto render country-brief.qmd --to docx        # Word only

The reference-doc option in the Word format lets you apply a branded Word template. Create a bka-template.docx with BK Advisors fonts, colors, and headers, and Quarto will apply that styling to every rendered document. This is how you produce client-ready Word documents directly from code.


Parameterized Reports

# YAML header with parameters:
#
# ---
# title: "MNH Country Brief"
# params:
#   country: "Ethiopia"
# ---

# In the document body, filter everything by the parameter:
#
# ```{r}
# country_data <- facility_data |>
#   filter(country == params$country)
# ```
#
# All subsequent analysis, charts, and inline R use `country_data`
# instead of `facility_data`.

# Render for a specific country from the terminal:
# quarto render country-brief.qmd -P country:"Tanzania"

# Loop to render for ALL countries (run in R):
countries <- c("Ethiopia", "Tanzania", "Malawi", "Kenya", "Rwanda")

for (cntry in countries) {
  quarto::quarto_render(
    input = "country-brief.qmd",
    output_file = paste0("brief-", tolower(cntry), ".html"),
    execute_params = list(country = cntry)
  )
}

# This produces:
# brief-ethiopia.html
# brief-tanzania.html
# brief-malawi.html
# brief-kenya.html
# brief-rwanda.html

Parameterized reports are the ultimate productivity tool for consulting. One template, five countries, five polished briefs — generated in seconds. When the next quarter’s data arrives, you re-run the loop and all five briefs update. This is the power of combining R with Quarto: your analysis, visualization, and communication are all in one reproducible pipeline.

title: Chapter 9: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Key Takeaways

Quarto unifies code, narrative, and output. A single .qmd file produces a complete document — no more copying charts into Word and manually updating numbers.
Inline R eliminates manual errors. Every number in your narrative is computed from the data. When the data changes, the narrative changes with it.
Parameterized reports scale your work. Write one template, render it for every country, region, or quarter. This is how a small consulting team produces deliverables at the speed of a large one.

title: Chapter 9: Reproducible Reports with Quarto
text:
Looking Ahead
This chapter completes the R for Management Consultants course. You now have the skills to:

Import, transform, and visualize MNH data in R
Build publication-quality charts with consistent branding
Compute descriptive statistics and interpret regression results
Produce automated, reproducible reports for clients

The natural next step is the Inference and Intervention course, where you will apply causal modeling to the data analysis skills you have built here. The question shifts from “What does the data show?” to “What should we do about it?” — using directed acyclic graphs, do-calculus, and intervention analysis to move from correlation to actionable causal claims.
